title: Simple Reflex Agents
concept: Rule-based routing of inputs to actions, bypassing deep reasoning layers for fast execution.

exercises.prompt: Welcome to Agent Archetypes! To start: what is an AI 'agent' in the simplest sense?

options.text: A program that senses its surroundings and takes actions
A person who sells AI software door to door
A robot that can only sit still and do nothing

exercises.explain: An AI agent is any system that perceives its environment (through inputs) and acts on it. Over this unit, we'll meet different 'personality types' of agents, starting with the fastest and simplest.

cards.title: Touch a Hot Stove
cards.body: Your hand jerks back before you even think about it. No planning, no weighing options, just instant reaction. Some AI agents work exactly like that reflex.

cards.title: Simple Reflex Agent
cards.body: A simple reflex agent picks an action using fixed if-this-then-that rules based only on what it senses right now. It skips deep thinking to act fast.

cards.title: Like a Light Switch
cards.body: Flip up, light turns on. Flip down, it turns off. The switch never plans or remembers; it just maps the input straight to an action, every single time.

cards.title: The Thermostat
cards.body: IF room is too cold, THEN turn on heat. IF too warm, THEN turn it off. A thermostat reacts instantly to the current temperature using one simple rule, with no long-term reasoning.

cards.title: "Fast Means Smart"
cards.body: Not quite! Reflex agents are fast because they DON'T think deeply. They can't handle situations their rules never covered. Speed comes from skipping reasoning, not from being clever.

cards.title: Reflex vs. Thinker
cards.body: One route dashes straight from input to action. The other pauses to reason first. Reflex agents take the shortcut.

left.text: See input, fire matching rule, act now

right.text: See input, think, plan, then act

exercises.prompt: What does a simple reflex agent use to decide its action?

options.text: Fixed if-this-then-that rules based on the current input
A long memory of every past event
A detailed plan for the next ten steps
A guess about how other agents feel

exercises.explain: Simple reflex agents map the current perception directly to an action through preset rules. No memory, no planning, just an instant rule match.

exercises.prompt: True or False: A simple reflex agent bypasses deep reasoning so it can react quickly.
exercises.explain: That's the whole point. By skipping heavy reasoning and jumping straight from input to a rule's action, the agent responds fast.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank about how reflex agents work.
exercises.sentence: A simple reflex agent routes each input straight to an action using fixed rules.
exercises.explain: Reflex agents rely on fixed rules to connect an input to an action, no memory or dreaming involved.

exercises.prompt: Which description of a reflex agent was written by an AI?

options.text: A simple reflex agent maps the current perception to an action via condition-action rules, prioritizing rapid execution over deliberative reasoning.
my thermostat clicks on when its cold lol thats basically it
The switch. Up light. Down dark. Very fast!!

exercises.explain: Option A has the polished, textbook-style phrasing ('condition-action rules', 'deliberative reasoning') that AI often produces. The others sound casual and human.

exercises.prompt: A smoke alarm blares the instant it senses smoke, without checking whether it's toast or a real fire. Which agent type is it, and why?

options.text: A simple reflex agent, because it maps a sensed input straight to an action with a fixed rule and no reasoning
A reasoning agent, because it carefully plans whether to sound
Not an agent, because it uses electricity
A memory-based agent, because it recalls past fires

exercises.explain: The alarm senses smoke and instantly triggers its rule (IF smoke THEN sound) with no planning or memory. That fast, rule-based routing from input to action is exactly what makes it a simple reflex agent. The trade-off: it can't tell burnt toast from danger, showing why speed comes at the cost of deeper reasoning.
